islamic-tolerance.script: Islamic Tolerance. The domes and minarets of a great mosque stand silhouetted against the sunset before you. Islam came both to unite and to protect, and the Holy Quran itself proclaims freedom of conscience. In chapter eighteen, verse thirty, it declares: "Proclaim: This is the truth from your Lord; then let him who will, believe, and let him who will, disbelieve." And in chapter two, verse two hundred and fifty-seven: "There is no compulsion in religion." Consider first Arab unity. After the demise of the Prophet Muhammad, peace and blessings be upon him, in six hundred and thirty-two, he was succeeded by the Rightly-Guided Caliphs. Arabia was for the first time unified. It entered battle with both the Romans and the Persians, and shocked the world by securing victory over them. Yet with that victory came a remarkable religious respect. Governance of the Persian capital was given to none other than Salman Al-Farsi. Muslim rule, especially in its first three centuries, was tolerant and protective of its religious minorities; the spirit of Cyrus was enlarged and perfected under Islam. For instance, in the tenth century, fire-temples were still to be found in almost every province of Persia. In Muslim Spain, the Jews had been greatly persecuted by the Christian Visigoths; under Muslim rule, they experienced a cultural renaissance that lasted for centuries. The tenth-century Andalusian Caliph Abd Al-Rahman the Third even had a Jewish physician as his foreign minister, and a Christian bishop as his envoy. But why did so many embrace the new faith? Sir Thomas Arnold, in his book The Preaching of Islam, published in eighteen ninety-six, explains how conversions to Islam were organic and gradual in newly Muslim lands. Yet many Persians were particularly swift in embracing Islam. As he writes: "… the Muslim creed was most eagerly welcomed by the townsfolk, the industrial classes and the artisans, whose occupations made them impure according to the Zoroastrian creed, because in the pursuance of their trade or occupations they defiled fire, earth or water, and who thus, outcasts in the eyes of the law and treated with scant consideration in consequence, embraced with eagerness a creed that made them at once free men, and equal in a brotherhood of faith… For the Persian could find in the Qur'an many of the fundamental doctrines of his old faith, though in a rather different form: he would meet again Ahuramazda and Ahriman under the names of Allah and Iblis; the creation of the world in six periods; the angels and the demons; the story of the primitive innocence of man; the resurrection of the body and the doctrine of heaven and hell. Even in the details of daily worship there were similarities to be found and the followers of Zoroaster when they adopted Islam were enjoined by their new faith to pray five times a day just as they had been by the Avesta."

verses.text: Proclaim: This is the truth from your Lord; then let him who will, believe, and let him who will, disbelieve.
There is no compulsion in religion

… the Muslim creed was most eagerly welcomed by the townsfolk, the industrial classes and the artisans, whose occupations made them impure according to the Zoroastrian creed, because in the pursuance of their trade or occupations they defiled fire, earth or water, and who thus, outcasts in the eyes of the law and treated with scant consideration in consequence, embraced with eagerness a creed that made them at once free men, and equal in a brotherhood of faith… For the Persian could find in the Qur'ān many of the fundamental doctrines of his old faith, though in a rather different form: he would meet again Ahuramazda and Ahriman under the names of Allah and Iblis; the creation of the world in six periods; the angels and the demons; the story of the primitive innocence of man; the resurrection of the body and the doctrine of heaven and hell. Even in the details of daily worship there were similarities to be found and the followers of Zoroaster when they adopted Islam were enjoined by their new faith to pray five times a day just as they had been by the Avesta.

persian-renaissance.script: Persian Renaissance. Above you glows a turquoise-tiled Persian dome. Consider first the Persian Muslims. Successive Persian empires spread far beyond the province of Fars and the borders of modern Iran. They covered parts of Arabia, Central Asia, the Levant, Afghanistan, the Indian subcontinent, and the Caucasus. All these peoples were influenced by Persian culture, language and administration; thus, many were considered Persianate without being ethnically Iranian. These peoples embraced Islam wholesale, and Persian became the language of scholarship and elite culture in many parts of the Islamic world. Consider next the matter of Sunni and Shia. Though today Iran is associated with Shiism, this was not the case for much of its history. Before the sixteenth century, most Iranians were Sunni. When the Safavid dynasty made Twelver Shiism the state religion in fifteen hundred and one, Sunni Islam was suppressed, and Shiism then became entrenched in Iran and much of nearby Azerbaijan. Most of the rest of the historically Persianate lands remained majority-Sunni. Yet both Sunni and Shia Muslims believed in the advent of the Imam Mahdi — a great reformer of Islam in the Latter Days. As we shall see, this concept is deeply linked to the past and future of Persia in Islam. Persians contributed considerably to the cause of Islam, and a small sample of them stands before you. There is Imam Abu Hanifa, founder of the Hanafi school of Islamic jurisprudence, of the eighth century, in modern Iraq. Imam Bukhari, the leading compiler of ahadith, of the ninth century, in modern Uzbekistan. Imam Muslim, another leading compiler of ahadith, of the ninth century, in modern Iran. Abu Bakr Al-Razi, a leading Islamic physician and shaper of modern medical culture, of the ninth and tenth centuries, in modern Iran. Al-Khwarizmi, the father of algebra and transmitter of the Hindu-Arabic numeral system, of the eighth and ninth centuries, in modern Uzbekistan. Al-Farabi, a leading Muslim philosopher, of the tenth century, in modern Kazakhstan. Al-Biruni, a polymath thinker and the author of an authoritative account of Indian ethnography, of the tenth and eleventh centuries, in modern Uzbekistan. Ibn Sina, the defining leading physician of the Muslim world and a defining philosophical mind, of the tenth and eleventh centuries, in modern Uzbekistan. Al-Ghazali, a scholar-saint who blended Sufism with Islamic orthodoxy, of the eleventh and twelfth centuries, in modern Iran. Omar Khayyam, polymath scientist, thinker and poet, of the eleventh and twelfth centuries, in modern Iran. Sheikh Abdul Qadir al-Jilani, a Sufi founder and Saint of Islam, from modern Iran. And Jalal Al-Din Rumi, the great Sufi saint and poet, of the thirteenth century, from modern Central Asia and Turkey. The renaissance of Persia became a gift to the whole world.

o-sons-of-persia.script: O Sons of Persia. Before you is a short but stirring revelation given to Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad, peace be upon him. Twice it repeats a single command, and then names those it addresses: "Hold fast to unity. Hold fast to unity. O sons of Persia." It is a call across the centuries to the people of Persia, urging them to hold together in faith. As this final section will reveal, that call was bound up with the coming of a reformer of Persian origin, the Promised Messiah, to whom we now turn.

verses.ref: Revelation of Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad
verses.text: Hold fast to unity. Hold fast to unity. O sons of Persia.

the-promised-messiah.script: The Promised Messiah. You are entering the final section of Prophecy and Persia: The Promised Messiah, the Imam Mahdi of Persian origin. Before you is a portrait of a bearded man in a turban: Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad. Having journeyed from Cyrus of Persia, through the rise of Islam and Persia's place within it, we come at last to the awaited reformer of Persian descent. Let the final part of our story begin.
